books.title: Everyone Else Seems to Manage
books.subtitle: For the sandwich-generation parent measuring her depletion against the friends who somehow handle it all without complaining — and always coming up short
books.desc: Everyone else seems to manage. Your friends care for their parents and raise their kids and still look rested, still say yes, still don't snap. And here you are, fraying, certain you're the only one who can't hold it together. This is a book for the parent who keeps measuring her limits against people who never show theirs.
books.long_desc:
Everyone else seems to manage. You're the only one drowning.

That's how it feels. Your friend visits her father three times a week and still makes it to spin class. Your sister handles your mother's appointments without ever sounding tired. The other parents at pickup ask 'how do you do it all?' as if you have an answer, and you smile, because the truth — that you're barely holding the line, that you said yes again when everything in you was screaming no — feels like a failing only you have.

This book is for the sandwich-generation parent who measures her own depletion against everyone who looks like they've got it figured out, and always comes up short.

You'll come to recognize the comparison for the trap it is. How you weigh your worst, most exhausted hour against other people's edited best. How 'she manages, so I should be able to' becomes the stick you beat your own limits with. How watching everyone else say yes makes your own no feel like weakness instead of wisdom. You'll see the way you hide your fraying so as not to lose the comparison — and how that hiding leaves you certain you're uniquely failing, alone in a struggle that half the people around you are also hiding. And in the warm middle of these pages, you'll feel the relief of setting the measuring stick down: of drawing your line where your life actually needs it, not where you imagine someone calmer would draw theirs.

This book will not help you win the comparison. It won't teach you to do more with less, or hand you the secret the rested ones supposedly know. That's the point. There is no version of you that handles a fading parent and a needing child and the whole sandwiched middle without it costing something. The ones who look like they manage are paying too — just somewhere you can't see.

You are not behind. You are not the only one. You've just been holding your tired, unedited life up against everyone else's good-enough day, and calling the gap a flaw.

In the pause before you go back in, this book lets you stop comparing long enough to remember: the line goes where you need it. Not where she put hers.

Book 3 of the Stolen Moment Press boundaries series.
books.angle: They post the caregiving. You're living it. Of course you can't keep up with a highlight reel.
books.author: Jonah Santos
keywords: feeling like a failure as a parent

books.title: Before You Even Say No
books.subtitle: For the sandwich-generation parent whose whole body braces the second the phone lights up — when the alarm goes off before there's anything to be afraid of
books.desc: You feel the dread before you know what it's about. Mom's name on the screen, your kid's door clicking shut, a text you haven't opened yet — and already your stomach has dropped and your jaw has set. This is a book for the parent whose body sounds the alarm long before there is anything to actually decide.
books.long_desc:
The phone lights up with your mother's name, and your whole body braces before you've even read the words.

You know this feeling. Not the emergency itself — the bracing for one. The held breath in the driveway before you go inside. The way your shoulders climb when you hear footsteps on the stairs. You are not afraid of any single thing. You are afraid all the time, of nothing you can name, because for years now the next ask has always been coming, and your body learned to stay ready.

This book is for the sandwich-generation parent caught between a parent who is fading and a child who still needs you, the one whose alarm never quite goes quiet.

You'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. How the dread arrives before the request does — and how you say yes just to make the dread stop, not because you ever decided to. How 'are you okay?' from your mother lands in your chest like something sounding. How you brace for the blow-up that, most days, never comes. How the line you meant to draw dissolves the instant your body floods, because you can't think clearly with the dread roaring. You'll feel the cost of living pre-flinched, always one ask ahead of yourself. And slowly, in the warm middle of these pages, you'll find the quiet underneath the alarm — the still pocket where you can tell a real emergency from an old echo, and answer from there instead.

This book will not teach you to silence your feelings or to stop caring. It will not promise that the next call won't come. There is no script here that makes you untouchable, no five-step system that makes the dread go still for good. That's not the point. The point is the half-second you get back — between the phone lighting up and your hand reaching for it — where you remember the choice was always yours.

Nothing here asks you to do more. You are already doing everything. This is the few stolen minutes where you get to do less, and find out you were never actually in danger.

You're allowed to feel the alarm and not obey it.

Book 1 of the Stolen Moment Press boundaries series.
books.angle: Your shoulders rose before you read the message. That's the false alarm, not the emergency.
books.author: Jonah Santos
keywords: how to set boundaries with aging parents
boundaries for adult children of aging parents

books.title: The One Who Never Needs Anything
books.subtitle: For the sandwich-generation parent who believes that wanting anything back makes them selfish — and has spent a lifetime overdoing it to prove otherwise
books.desc: You are the one who shows up. The reliable one, the capable one, the one who never makes it about herself. And underneath all of it sits a quiet, ancient belief: that if you ever stopped giving, they'd see what you really are. This is a book for the parent who has been overworking a single hidden shame her whole life.
books.long_desc:
Somewhere a long time ago, you decided that the way to be loved was to never need anything.

So you became the good one. The capable daughter, the steady parent, the one who handles it. You answer every call. You absorb every mood. You say yes before the question is finished, and you call it love, and partly it is — but partly it's something older and quieter. A belief that if you ever drew a line, ever said 'not this time,' they would finally see the selfish, unlovable thing you've always feared you are.

This book is for the sandwich-generation parent who has spent a lifetime overdoing it to outrun a shame she can't quite name.

You'll come to recognize the belief that's been running you. How 'I should be able to handle this' is really 'something is wrong with me if I can't.' How you over-give not from abundance but from terror of being found out. How a boundary feels less like a limit and more like a confession — proof of the defect you've worked your whole life to cover. You'll watch the way you flinch at your own needs, apologize for taking up space, perform the endless competence that keeps the shame just out of sight. And in the warm turn of these pages, you'll meet the cost of it — the exhaustion of carrying a self you believe you have to hide — and feel, maybe for the first time, that the line you were so afraid to draw is not evidence of your selfishness. It's evidence that you're a person.

This book will not hand you affirmations to repeat until you believe them. It will not promise that the shame lifts in a weekend, or that you'll wake up one morning finally feeling like enough. That's not what this is. There is no fix here, because there was never anything in you that needed fixing — only a story you were too young to question when it first took hold.

You don't have to earn the right to rest. You don't have to be useful to be allowed in the room.

In the quiet after everyone's asleep, when no one needs you and there's no one left to please, this book sits with you and says the thing no one said back then: you were always good enough. The overworking was never the proof. It was the wound.

Book 2 of the Stolen Moment Press boundaries series.
books.angle: You were never the difficult one. That was the whole problem.
books.author: Jonah Santos

books.title: The Person You Used to Have Time to Be
books.subtitle: For the parent grieving a self that got buried under everyone else's needs — and the slow loss that no one thinks to name
books.desc: No one died, but something is gone. The version of you with room in her life. The friendships that quietly thinned. The parent your mother used to be. This is a small book for the grief that hides inside burnout — the losses too ordinary to mourn out loud — and for letting yourself finally feel them in the quiet after bedtime.
books.long_desc:
No one died. That's what makes it so hard to say out loud. But you've been grieving for a long time now, quietly, in the spaces between everything you have to do. You miss the person you used to be — the one with time, with energy, with a life that had her own name on it. You miss friendships that didn't end so much as fade, because there was never a free evening to keep them. You miss the parent your mother used to be, before the roles reversed and you became the one holding her steady.

This book is for you. The one in the middle, raising children while caring for parents, carrying a kind of loss that no one sends a card for. You are not ungrateful and you are not falling apart. You are simply mourning what burnout quietly takes — the slow disappearance of a self, the small deaths that happen without a funeral, the future you'd half-pictured that isn't going to look the way you thought.

You'll come to recognize the grief underneath the exhaustion. The way letting go isn't something you've refused to do but something you've never been given the time to do. The way you brace against feeling it because feeling it might undo you, and there's no one to take over while you fall apart. The way the loss comes up in the quiet after bedtime, when the house finally goes still and there's no task left to outrun it with.

This is not a book about closure, and it will not hand you five stages to move through. Nothing here will tell you to be grateful for what remains, or to find the silver lining, or to bounce back. It will not promise that the grief lifts on a schedule. It will not fix what was lost.

What it offers instead is permission to grieve at all — to admit that something real is gone, even though everyone is still here, even though you're supposed to be holding it together. To let one stolen moment be for mourning instead of managing. To stop pretending the losses don't count just because they were quiet.

You are allowed to miss who you were. You are allowed to set it down and feel it, just for now.

Book 2 of the Stolen Moment Press Sandwich Generation Burnout series.
books.angle: You miss someone, and it's you.
books.author: Bea Raman

books.title: Everyone Needs Something From You at Once
books.subtitle: For the parent caught between aging parents and growing kids — when the demands all arrive in the same hour and there's no room left to think
books.desc: It all lands at the same time — the school email, the pharmacy on hold, the kid who needs you now, the parent who needed you yesterday. This is a small book for the parent whose day has no gaps left in it, and who can't remember the last thought that was only theirs. It won't add a single task. It just makes a little room.
books.long_desc:
It happens in a single hour. The school sends an email that needs answering. The pharmacy puts you on hold. One kid is crying in the next room and the other needs a permission slip signed by morning. Your mother calls, and you can hear in her voice that something has changed again. It all arrives at once, and there is no space between any of it — no pause to decide what matters, no quiet to think in. You just react, and react, and react, until the day is gone and you couldn't tell anyone what you actually did.

This book is for you. The one in the middle. The one raising children and steadying parents in the same week, sometimes the same afternoon. You are not disorganized and you are not weak. You are simply being asked for more than any one person can hold, all at the same time, with nothing left over — and your mind has started to flood because the flood is real.

You'll come to recognize the shape of the overwhelm. The way too much arriving at once isn't a character flaw but a capacity problem — too many things, too few hands, no margin. The way you've started skimming your own life because there's no time to read it. The way a full inbox of a mind makes even small choices feel impossible, and how the pile is loudest in the few minutes you finally get to yourself, in the car, in the dark, before you go back in.

This is not a productivity book. There is no system here, no morning routine, no app, no five steps to a calmer you. Nothing in these pages will ask you to do more or be more or optimize a single thing. It will not promise to fix the load. The load is not yours to fix tonight.

What it offers instead is smaller and truer: a way to set the flood down for the length of one stolen moment, without earning it first, without anyone noticing it's gone. You don't have to empty the whole list. You just have to find the one small pocket of quiet that is yours, and let yourself be in it.

You are allowed to stop, even for a minute, even with everything undone.

Book 1 of the Stolen Moment Press Sandwich Generation Burnout series.
books.angle: Everyone is talking and none of it can wait.
books.author: Bea Raman
keywords: parenting and caring for elderly parents

books.title: Always Listening for the Next Thing to Go Wrong
books.subtitle: For the parent who can never fully clock out — the mind that keeps scanning for the call, the fever, the fall, even in the rare quiet
books.desc: Part of you is always on watch. Listening for the cry down the hall, the phone that rings at the wrong hour, the small sign that someone is about to need you. This is a small book for the parent whose mind never goes off duty — and for the radical idea that you're allowed to stop scanning for a few stolen minutes, and just be off.
books.long_desc:
Some part of you is always listening. Even now, even in the rare quiet, you're half-tuned to the hallway in case a child stirs, half-braced for the phone in case it's the care home, the hospital, the bad-news hour. You scan faces for the change you've learned to catch early. You sleep with one ear open. You can't remember the last time you were fully, genuinely off duty — because the watching is how you've kept everyone safe, and it has never once switched off.

This book is for you. The one in the middle, keeping a child and a parent both in view at the same time, running a watch that never gets to rest. You are not paranoid and you are not controlling. You are simply the person who has learned, through real emergencies, that vigilance is love — and now the vigilance won't let go, even when there's nothing to catch.

You'll come to recognize the watcher in you. The part of the mind that treats every silence as the calm before something, that mistakes rest for danger because resting once meant missing something. The way you keep watch for trouble that almost never comes, and how the watching costs you the very quiet it's standing guard over. The way it gets loudest in the stolen moments — the pause in the car, the minute after bedtime — when there's finally nothing to do and your mind refuses to believe it.

This is not a book about control, and it will not teach you to manage your mind into stillness. There's no breathing trick here that promises to switch the alarm off for good, no worry to optimize away. It will not tell you to stop caring, and it will not promise that nothing bad will happen. It cannot undo the part of you that learned to stay ready.

What it offers instead is something gentler: a way to let the watch stand down for the length of one stolen moment. To trust, for just a minute, that you can be off — that the world will hold while you set the watching down. To let a small pocket of quiet be quiet, instead of a warning.

You are allowed to stop listening for a moment. You are allowed to be off duty, even briefly, even now.

Book 3 of the Stolen Moment Press Sandwich Generation Burnout series.
books.angle: Even the silence feels like it's about to break.
books.author: Bea Raman

books.title: Grieving a Mother Who Is Still Alive
books.subtitle: Mourning the parent who used to be the one who knew what to do — and letting go of who you thought you'd get to be
books.desc: She is still here, and you are already grieving — the mother who used to be the one who knew what to do, the future you pictured, the version of caregiving you can no longer sustain. This is a book for the parent in the middle, mourning losses that have no funeral and no permission. It will not tell you to look on the bright side. It will sit in the quiet with you and let the loss be real.
books.long_desc:
No one warns you about this kind of grief. The kind where the person is still alive — still in the next room, still on the phone — but the parent you knew is slipping away one small thing at a time. She forgets the name of the street you grew up on. He asks the same question a fourth time. The mother who used to be the one you called when you didn't know what to do now needs you to know what to do, and something in you is breaking that has no name and no casserole and no one standing at the door to say they're sorry for your loss.

This book is for the Gen X parent caught in the middle — raising your own children while you watch a parent decline, grieving in advance and grieving in private. You are mourning more than a person. You are mourning the future you assumed you'd have, the version of yourself who would get to rest someday, the relationship you wanted and won't get to finish. And underneath all of it, you may be grieving the parent you can no longer be to your own kids, because you have so little left to give.

Here you will come to recognize the losses you've been carrying without a word for them. The slow goodbye that started before anyone acknowledged it. The guilt that hides inside grief — the part of you that is tired, that has had moments of wishing it were over, and then drowns in shame for thinking it. You will learn that letting go is not the same as giving up, and that you are allowed to release what you cannot carry without releasing the love underneath it.

This book will not rush you toward acceptance. It will not tell you she's still here so be grateful, or that everything happens for a reason, or hand you five stages to move through on a schedule. There is no fixing a loss like this. There is only being allowed to feel it — in the parked car, in the quiet after bedtime, in the stolen minutes when you finally let yourself stop being strong.

So let it be heavy. You are not failing because this hurts. You are grieving someone you love while she is still here to be loved, and that is one of the hardest things a person can be asked to do. You are allowed to set it down for a moment. You are allowed to be the one who is held.

Book 2 of the Stolen Moment Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You are losing her slowly, and no one sends a casserole for that.
books.author: Opal Janssen
keywords: sandwich generation caring for aging parents

books.title: Pulled in Every Direction but Your Own
books.subtitle: For the sandwich-generation parent giving to a parent and a child with nothing left for themselves
books.desc: Your mother's doctor, your kid's text, the work email, the dishwasher beeping — it all lands in the same five minutes, and somewhere in there, so do you. This is a book for the parent caught in the middle, giving from a cup that emptied a long time ago. It will not hand you a system. It will sit with you in the flood and help you find the one breath that is still yours.
books.long_desc:
It happens in a single hour. Your phone lights up with your father's pharmacy on one line and your daughter's school on the other. The crockpot needs starting, the form was due yesterday, and your own body has been sending you signals you keep promising to deal with later. Everyone needs you at once, and there is no version of you left over to need anything back.

This book is for the Gen X parent in the middle of the sandwich — raising kids who still depend on you while caring for parents who used to care for you. You are not disorganized. You are not weak. You are a person who has been pouring from an empty cup for so long you forgot the cup was ever yours to fill. Compassion fatigue is not a character flaw. It is what happens when the well runs dry and the bucket keeps coming down anyway.

In these pages you will come to recognize the particular shape of being overwhelmed by love — not too little caring, but far too much arriving all at once, with no room left between the demands to think, to breathe, to be a person and not just a pair of hands. You will see the cost you have been quietly paying: the way you snap and then hate yourself for it, the calls you let go to voicemail, the part of you that has started to go numb just to get through the day. You will find a few honest minutes — in the parked car, in the quiet after bedtime, in the pause before you open the door — that belong to no one but you.

This book will not optimize your day. It will not give you a morning routine or a color-coded calendar or one more thing to feel behind on. There is no fix here, because you are not broken. The flood is real, and you cannot drain an ocean by trying harder. What you can do is stop trying to hold all of it in your two hands at the same time, and learn — slowly, gently — that setting something down is not the same as letting someone fall.

So set it down. The list will still be there. For the length of these pages, no one needs anything from you that you cannot give. You are allowed to be the one who is tended to, even if only for a moment. Especially then.

Book 1 of the Stolen Moment Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You filled every cup but yours, and now there is nothing left to pour.
books.author: Opal Janssen
keywords: caring for elderly parents and kids

books.title: The Part of You That Never Stops Checking
books.subtitle: For the caregiver whose mind never quiets — reading every mood, counting every pill, bracing for the fall before it comes
books.desc: You read your mother's voice for the first sign of confusion. You watch your teenager's face across the dinner table. You lie awake doing the medication math one more time. This is a book for the parent whose mind never quiets — always scanning, always braced, always the one who has to catch it. It will not give you one more thing to check. It will help you find the part of you that is allowed to stop watching, even for a breath.
books.long_desc:
You hear your father's footsteps on the stairs and your whole body tenses for the sound of a fall that doesn't come. You scan your mother's face on the video call for the flicker of confusion you're afraid to find. You watch your kid push food around their plate and run the quiet calculation: is this normal, or is this the thing I'm supposed to catch? Your mind never quiets. It is always scanning, always one step ahead of a disaster, because you have decided — without ever deciding — that if you stop watching, something will slip through, and it will be your fault.

This book is for the Gen X parent in the middle, the one holding two generations in your peripheral vision at all times. You are the one who notices first. You hold the medications, the moods, the missed calls, the subtle changes only you would catch. And the watchfulness that started as love has quietly swallowed everything else — it follows you into the shower, into the dark, into the few minutes that were supposed to be yours.

In these pages you will come to recognize the watcher in you: the part that scans for trouble that mostly never comes, that mistakes constant checking for keeping everyone safe, that cannot rest because rest feels like letting go of something you are not allowed to drop. You'll see what the endless scanning has cost — your sleep, your attention, the ability to sit in a quiet moment without your mind reaching for the next thing to check. And you'll begin to understand that being braced every hour is not the same as keeping anyone alive.

This book will not hand you a cleverer way to keep watch, or promise you control over outcomes you were never holding in the first place. There is no watching hard enough to prevent everything, and you have been trying to anyway. What you can do is let the watching rest — for one breath, in the parked car, in the quiet after bedtime — and learn that the world does not end the moment you stop bracing for it.

So let your shoulders drop. You are allowed to not be scanning right now. You are allowed a few minutes where nothing is yours to catch. The watchfulness carried you here; it does not get to keep you forever.

Book 3 of the Stolen Moment Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: Even in the few minutes that are yours, you are still listening for the fall.
books.author: Opal Janssen
keywords: caregiver can't relax or switch off

books.title: They Make Holding It Together Look Easy
books.subtitle: For the parent sure every other family runs smoother than theirs
books.desc: Every other parent seems to say no so cleanly, hold their limits so easily, juggle the job and the kids without coming apart. You watch them and you feel it: you're the only one struggling this much. This book is for the parent who measures their own fraying boundaries against everyone who looks effortless, and never measures up.
books.long_desc:
You see it everywhere once you start looking. The parent at pickup who says no thanks, we're full this weekend without a flicker of apology. The coworker who logs off at five and doesn't think about it again. The friend whose kids, whose job, whose calm somehow all fit, while yours spill over the edges of every single day. And the quiet verdict lands again: everyone else makes it look easy. You're the only one who can't hold the line.

This is for the working parent who has turned other people into a measuring stick and keeps coming up short. The one who scrolls, and watches, and compares their own exhausted, boundary-less Tuesday against the curated calm of everyone who seems to have it figured out. You're not lazy and you're not failing. You've just pointed a sharp, tired attention at the wrong target — at yourself, against a field of people whose private struggles you will never see.

In the stolen minutes this book asks of you — the pause in the car, the small still pocket between drop-off and the workday closing in — you'll come to recognize the comparison for the trap it is. You'll see how the effortless ones are mostly just better at hiding the same fraying you feel. You'll notice how every measurement leaves you with less, not more — less certainty about your own limits, less permission to draw a line that's yours and not theirs. And you'll feel what it's like to stop asking how does everyone else do it and start asking the only question that was ever yours to answer: where do I draw the line, for my family, in my one actual life?

This book will not help you win the comparison. That's the point. It won't optimize you into the effortless parent you think everyone else already is. It won't promise the watching stops. What it offers is quieter and kinder: the recognition that your boundaries were never supposed to match anyone else's, and that the line you keep failing to find was only ever invisible because you were looking at the wrong life to find it.

You don't have to hold it the way they seem to. You don't have to be effortless to be enough. The few minutes you steal to read this belong to you alone — no one to measure against, nothing to win.

Book 3 of the Stolen Moment Press Boundaries series.
books.angle: They all seem to know exactly where the line goes. You never do.
books.author: Jonah Santos
keywords: stop comparing yourself to other parents

books.title: The Yes That Costs You Everything
books.subtitle: For the working parent whose body braces before the word is even out of their mouth — the false alarm that turns every small ask into an emergency you must answer
books.desc: Someone asks for a little more of you and your whole system lights up like it's a crisis. You say yes before you've decided to, just to make the feeling stop. This is the book for the parent whose alarm goes off at the smallest request, and who has forgotten it's allowed to be quiet.
books.long_desc:
It's a Tuesday, and your phone buzzes. A text from the group chat. A coworker leaning into your doorway. Your kid calling your name from the other room while you're already mid-something. And before you've even read the words, your chest has gone tight, your jaw has set, and a quiet voice inside is already saying yes, of course, no problem — anything to make the bracing stop.

This is for the working parent whose body sounds the alarm before there's anything to be alarmed about. The one who treats a small request like an emergency, who answers every demand as if saying no would bring the whole thing crashing down. You've started to confuse the spike of urgency with proof that the thing actually matters that much. It usually doesn't. Your alarm system is just old, and loyal, and firing at shadows.

In the stolen minutes you'll spend here — the pause in the car before you go in, the quiet after the last small body is finally asleep — you'll come to recognize the false alarm for what it is. You'll feel how the yes leaves your mouth a half-second before your real answer does. You'll notice the cost you've been quietly paying: the resentment you can't explain, the depletion that sleep doesn't touch, the sense that your own life is something you keep volunteering away. And you'll learn what it feels like to let the alarm ring without leaping to answer it — to stand at the threshold of a request and ask, just once, where do I actually draw the line here?

This book will not hand you a script for the perfect no. It won't optimize your boundaries or turn you into someone who never flinches. It won't promise that the bracing disappears. What it offers is smaller and truer: the recognition that the alarm is not an order, that a half-second of pause is yours to take, and that you are allowed to feel the spike and still choose your own answer.

You don't have to earn the right to hesitate. You don't have to be calm to be allowed to say no. The few minutes you steal to read this are already a boundary — a small still pocket in a full day that belongs to no one but you.

Book 1 of the Stolen Moment Press Boundaries series.
books.angle: Your chest tightens before you've even said yes.
books.author: Jonah Santos
keywords: how to say no without guilt
setting boundaries at work and home

books.title: A Good Parent Wouldn't Need a No
books.subtitle: For the working parent who believes the limit they need is proof of a flaw — and overgives to cover a defect no one else can see
books.desc: You don't just struggle to set a boundary — you're convinced that needing one means something is wrong with you. So you give past empty and hide the cost, sure that a good parent, a good worker, a good person wouldn't have a limit in the first place. This book is for the part of you that overworks to cover a defect that was never there.
books.long_desc:
There's a thought you've never quite said out loud. It lives underneath the late nights, the unanswered needs of your own, the way you say of course before anyone's even finished asking. The thought goes something like this: a good parent wouldn't need this. A good parent wouldn't be this depleted, this resentful, this close to the edge over something so small. So you give past empty, and you hide how empty you are, because the alternative is admitting the flaw you're certain is yours.

This is for the working parent who carries boundaries as a shame, not a skill. The one who believes their own limit is evidence of a defect — that the need for rest, for room, for a no, is proof they're failing at the one thing they're supposed to be good at. You don't overgive because you have nothing better to do. You overgive to cover something. To prove, one yes at a time, that you're not the inadequate person you secretly fear you are.

In the stolen minutes this book asks of you — the quiet after bedtime, the breath caught between one demand and the next — you'll come to recognize how shame has been doing your negotiating for you. You'll see how it disguises a perfectly human limit as a personal failure. You'll feel the difference between I can't do this and I'm bad for not being able to do this. And slowly, at your own pace, you'll stand at the threshold of a no and discover that the defect you've been overworking to hide was never a defect at all. It was just a person, with edges, asking to be allowed to have them.

This book will not fix you, because you were never broken. It won't optimize your worth or hand you a cleaner, more deserving version of yourself to become. It won't promise the shame goes silent. What it offers is the slow, unglamorous recognition that needing a boundary is not a flaw to be covered — it's the most ordinary thing in the world, and it was always allowed.

You do not have to be a perfect parent to deserve a limit. You do not have to earn your own rest. The few minutes you steal to read this are not selfish. They are yours. They always were.

Book 2 of the Stolen Moment Press Boundaries series.
books.angle: You're sure a better parent wouldn't be this tired.
books.author: Jonah Santos

books.title: The Parent I Thought I'd Be
books.subtitle: For the working parent grieving quietly under the burnout — mourning the patience, the presence, and the person you ran out of before anyone noticed
books.desc: Underneath the exhaustion there is something you rarely let yourself feel: grief. For the parent you meant to be, the moments that blurred past, the version of you that had patience to spare. This is a book for the working parent who is not just tired, but quietly mourning, and has never been given permission to call it that.
books.long_desc:
Sometimes it catches you in the car, parked in the driveway, engine off, not ready to go in yet. A sudden ache you can't quite name. Not anger, not stress, something heavier and older. It's grief, though you may have never called it that, because who has time to grieve a life they're still living?

This is a book for the working parent carrying a loss no one acknowledges. You haven't lost a person. You've lost the parent you thought you'd be, the one with patience that didn't run dry by 6pm, who didn't snap over spilled milk, who was present instead of just present in the room. You've lost moments that blurred past while you were managing logistics. You've lost a version of yourself that had something left over at the end of the day. Burnout took these quietly, one ordinary Tuesday at a time, and you never got to mourn them.

Here you'll come to recognize the grief that lives underneath the tiredness. How burnout isn't only depletion, it's a series of small bereavements you've been too busy to feel. How the guilt you carry is often grief wearing a costume, because grief had nowhere to go. How letting go of who you thought you'd be is not giving up, but the only way to actually be here for who your child is now. You'll see why the ache shows up in the quiet after bedtime, in the pause before you walk through the door, in the stolen moments when you finally stop moving long enough to feel anything at all.

This book will not promise to give you that parent back. It won't tell you that with the right routine you'll become endlessly patient and present. There is no recovery plan here that ends with you restored to a self that the season of your life cannot sustain. What it offers instead is the permission to grieve what burnout took, gently, in the few minutes that are yours, so the loss can finally move through you instead of sitting on your chest.

You are allowed to miss who you were. That ache is love, not failure.

Book 2 of the Stolen Moment Press working parents burnout series.
books.angle: No one tells you that burnout is also a kind of loss.
books.author: Bea Raman
keywords: grieving the parent you wanted to be

books.title: Everything at Once, and It's Already Late
books.subtitle: For the working parent whose to-do list has no bottom — when too much arrives at the same time and there's no room left in you to hold any of it
books.desc: There is a moment, usually after bedtime, when the day finally stops moving and you realize you never had a chance. The emails, the laundry, the form that was due, the dinner that has to happen again tomorrow. This is a book for the working parent who is not disorganized and not lazy, just buried under more than any one person was built to carry at once.
books.long_desc:
It starts before your feet hit the floor. The day is already full when you wake into it, and by 8am you're behind on things you haven't even reached yet. The lunches, the drop-off, the meeting you didn't prepare for, the text from school, the thing you said you'd handle. It all arrives at the same time, and there is no room left in you to think, only to react.

This is a book for the working parent who is drowning in the volume of it. Not the parent who needs better habits or a cleaner calendar, but the one who has tried all of that and is still standing in the kitchen at 9pm with the counter full and nowhere to put any of it down. You are not failing at managing your life. Your life is asking more of you, all at once, than capacity allows, and your body has started to register that as a constant low alarm.

Here you'll come to recognize what overwhelm actually is. How it isn't the same as having too much to do, but the feeling of it all landing at once, with no space between. How it floods out the part of you that can prioritize, so everything feels equally urgent and nothing feels possible. How the to-do list isn't really a list anymore, it's a tide, and you've been trying to bail it with a teaspoon. You'll see why you freeze on the stair instead of getting up, and why that freeze is not weakness but a flooded system protecting itself.

This book will not hand you a new system. It will not teach you to do more in less time, or wake earlier, or finally get organized. There is no productivity hack here, because the problem was never your productivity. What it offers instead is recognition, and a few stolen minutes you don't have to earn, the small still pocket in a day that has no room in it, reclaimed without anyone noticing.

You are allowed to not catch up tonight. The flood does not require you to be its match.

Book 1 of the Stolen Moment Press working parents burnout series.
books.author: Bea Raman
keywords: too much to do as a parent
feeling overwhelmed as a working parent

books.title: The Shift That Never Quite Ends
books.subtitle: For the parent whose mind keeps working long after the kids are finally asleep
books.desc: The house is quiet. Everyone is safe. And your mind is still running the perimeter, checking the calendar, replaying the day, bracing for tomorrow. This is a book for the working parent whose vigilance never powers down, the one doing the invisible work of tracking everything so nothing falls, even when there's nothing left to catch.
books.long_desc:
The lights are off. The kids are breathing slow in the next room. Everyone is fine. And still, some part of you is awake at the controls, scanning. Did you sign the form. Is there milk. Is the meeting at nine or ten. Did your kid seem off today. Is something wrong that you missed. The day is over but you are not, because the part of you that watches never clocks out.

This is a book for the working parent who carries the invisible job of keeping track of everything. You're the one who knows where the shoes are, when the prescription runs out, which child needs the dentist, what's due Friday. You hold the whole map in your head, and you can't put it down, because if you stop watching, you're sure something will fall. So the scanning continues into the night, long past any threat, monitoring a world that is, for this one moment, actually okay.

Here you'll come to recognize the watcher in you. How hypervigilance is not the same as caring, even though it feels inseparable from it. How burnout keeps the alarm system running on high alert even when there's no fire, scanning for threats that never come. How the mental load isn't just the doing, it's the never-being-allowed-to-stop-tracking, the exhausting work of being the one who notices everything. You'll see why you can't fall asleep even when you're desperate for it, why your mind grabs the next worry the moment one is resolved, and why rest feels unsafe when you're the one holding it all together.

This book will not teach you to monitor more efficiently or finally get on top of it all. It won't promise that with the right planner your mind will quiet down on command. There is no system here that ends with you in perfect, watchful control, because the watching itself is the wound. What it offers instead is a way to let the perimeter go for a few stolen minutes, to set the map down where no one can see, and to let the part of you that's been on duty all day finally, briefly, rest.

You are allowed to stop scanning. The night does not need a guard.

Book 3 of the Stolen Moment Press working parents burnout series.
books.angle: Everyone's asleep. You're still on duty.
books.author: Bea Raman
keywords: can't switch off my brain parent

books.title: I Miss the Parent I Meant to Be
books.subtitle: For the parent grieving the warmth compassion fatigue quietly took — mourning the patient, present version of you and learning to set the guilt down
books.desc: Somewhere along the way the warmth went quiet. You still love them — you just can't feel it the way you used to, and the snappish, going-through-the-motions parent in the mirror isn't who you meant to be. This is for the working parent grieving that loss, and gently letting go of the guilt that came with it.
books.long_desc:
There's a particular ache that comes after bedtime. The house is finally quiet, and you stand in the doorway of their room watching them sleep, and you feel it: I love this child more than my own life — so why did I spend the whole day just trying to get through them? You remember a version of yourself who was patient. Who knelt down and listened. Who didn't measure the minutes until lights-out. You miss her. You miss him. And you have no idea where that person went.

This book is for the working parent who is grieving — not a death, but a quiet disappearance. The slow loss of the warmth you used to feel so easily. Compassion fatigue doesn't take your love; it takes your access to it. It leaves you caring just as much and feeling almost none of it, going through the right motions with a heart that's gone numb from overuse. And underneath the numbness sits the guilt, heavy and constant: that you're not the parent you promised yourself you'd be.

Here you'll come to recognize the shape of that grief — that what you're mourning is real, and that mourning it doesn't make you ungrateful. You'll see how the warmth went quiet: not because you stopped caring, but because a heart that pours out all day, every day, with nothing flowing back, eventually runs dry to protect itself. You'll learn the difference between the parent you are on your most depleted day and the parent you actually are. And in the few stolen minutes that are yours — the pause in the car, the quiet after the house goes still — you'll begin the gentlest thing of all: letting go of the version of you that the guilt insists you should have been, so there's finally room for the one who's actually here.

This book will not promise to give you that old self back, fixed and patient and endlessly warm by next week. It won't optimize your bedtimes or tell you to be more present through sheer willpower. Grief doesn't work that way, and you've been told to try harder for long enough. There is no fix here. There is only honesty, and company, and permission.

You are allowed to miss who you were. You are allowed to grieve it without it meaning you've failed them. The warmth is not gone for good — it's just been buried under everything you carry. And it comes back the same way you set anything heavy down: a few minutes at a time, when no one's asking for you.

Book 2 of the Stolen Moment Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You didn't lose your love for them. You lost the room to feel it.
books.author: Opal Janssen
keywords: grieving the parent I used to be

books.title: Nothing Left by the Time You Get to You
books.subtitle: For the parent who pours out all day and reaches the end with an empty cup
books.desc: By 6pm you've answered to a hundred people, and there's still dinner, homework, baths, and a small voice asking for you again. This is for the working parent who gives care all day and comes home to give more, quietly carrying nothing left. It won't hand you a system. It'll hand you a few minutes that are finally yours.
books.long_desc:
It usually hits in the doorway. You've just walked in from a day of being needed — by your boss, your inbox, your phone, the person who wouldn't stop talking — and before your coat is off, three more voices want you at once. Mom. Mama. Can you. The dog. The pan's burning. And somewhere underneath it all, a flat, frightening thought you'd never say out loud: I can't do this one more time tonight.

This book is for the working parent whose day is a flood of other people's needs, all arriving at the same time. Not the parent who doesn't care — the one who cares so much, for so many, that there's no room left inside to even hear yourself think. You're not cold. You're not failing your kids. You're at capacity, and you've been at capacity for so long you forgot there was ever any other way to feel.

Here you'll come to recognize what overwhelm actually is: not a flaw in you, but the simple math of too much landing in too small a space. You'll see how the flood works — how every demand feels equally urgent until you can't tell the burning pan from the bedtime story, and how the mind goes numb not because it's broken but because it's protecting you. You'll learn to find the one small still pocket in a full evening: the pause in the car before you go in, the ninety seconds behind the only locked door in the house, the breath you catch between one demand and the next. Not to do more with. To finally do nothing with.

This book will not give you a routine. It will not optimize your evenings or promise you a calmer, more grateful version of yourself by Friday. There is no morning ritual here, no checklist, no one more thing to add to a life that is already too full. That's the whole point. A depleted parent does not need another task. You need somewhere to set the load down for a minute, and someone to tell you that setting it down is allowed.

Nothing here asks you to give more. It only asks you to notice how much you already give, and to take back a few minutes that no one will miss but you.

You are allowed to be a person who ran out of room. That's not the end of your tenderness. It might be the beginning of getting it back.

Book 1 of the Stolen Moment Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: You didn't run out of love. You ran out of room.
books.author: Opal Janssen
keywords: exhausted mom no time for self

books.title: I Never Get to Clock Off
books.subtitle: For the parent whose mind never goes off duty — still scanning the next need, the next mood, the next thing about to go wrong, long after the house is asleep
books.desc: Even after lights-out, part of you is still on watch — listening for a cough, bracing for tomorrow, tracking everyone's mood before they've said a word. This is for the working parent whose mind never clocks off, who reads the room for a living and at home too. It won't make you stop caring. It'll show you how to stand down for a few stolen minutes.
books.long_desc:
The house is asleep. You should be too. But part of you is still on shift — ear half-cocked for a cough down the hall, mind already three moves into tomorrow morning, running the silent tally you never stop running: who's off today, who didn't eat, who needs the form signed, what's about to go wrong before it does. You gave care all day at work and all evening at home, and now, in the dark, the most exhausting part of you still won't go off duty. You can't remember the last time your mind was simply allowed to be quiet.

This book is for the working parent who lives as a watcher. The one who reads every room they walk into, anticipates every need before it's spoken, scans every face for the first flicker of a problem — at work, with the kids, in the marriage, everywhere, all the time. It's a kind of love. It's also a kind of exhaustion that no one can see, because the work happens entirely inside your head, and it never, ever clocks off.

Here you'll come to recognize the watcher for what it is: not vigilance you chose, but a caregiving mind that learned it could never afford to relax, and forgot how to stop. You'll see how the scanning works — how it hunts for threats that mostly never come, how it dresses itself up as 'just being responsible,' how it keeps you on call long after everyone else has been allowed to put the day down. You'll learn the difference between the watching that actually protects your kids and the watching that only wears you out. And in the few minutes that are honestly yours — the pause before you go inside, the still pocket after the house goes quiet — you'll practice the thing the watcher swears is impossible: letting your guard down, just for a moment, and trusting that the world will hold without you monitoring it.

This book will not switch the watching off like a light. It won't optimize your vigilance into something efficient, or promise you'll never scan a room again. The watcher came from love and it isn't going anywhere overnight, and any book that says otherwise hasn't sat where you're sitting. There is no off-switch here. There's something better: permission to stand down, a few minutes at a time, without it meaning you've stopped caring.

You are allowed to take your mind off duty. Not forever — just for now, just for these few stolen minutes that no one is asking anything of. The watch will still be there. You don't have to be the only one holding it every single second.

Book 3 of the Stolen Moment Press compassion fatigue series.
books.angle: Everyone else got to put the day down. Your mind is still on shift.
books.author: Opal Janssen
